docs.title: AMS+ Documentation
docs.text:
This site is the living source of truth for how AgencyBloc AMS+ works — written for product managers, support agents, and new hires.

It is maintained by an AI pipeline that monitors code changes and keeps content current, with every update reviewed by a human before it goes live.

docs.title: What you'll find here
docs.text: Section What it answers Explanation How a feature works and why it exists How-to guides Step-by-step guides for specific tasks Tutorials End-to-end walkthroughs for new hires Reference Field definitions and lookup tables

docs.title: Where to start
docs.text:
• New to AMS+? Start with the Commissions overview.
  • Looking for something specific? Use the search bar at the top.
  • Notice something wrong? Open a GitHub issue or reach out to your business-area owner.

About this site

Content is generated and updated by the AMS+ docs pipeline — an AI agent that reads code changes, Jira tickets, and Confluence pages, then proposes updates for human review. Every page shows its last-verified date.

docs.title: Explanation
docs.text: Deep-dives into how AMS+ business areas work and why they work that way. Written for product managers, support agents, and new hires — not engineers.

docs.title: Business areas
docs.text:
• Commissions — how commission splits, schedules, and payments work
  • Policies — the policy lifecycle, carrier linkage, and renewal rules
  • Compliance — E&O requirements and CMS rules for Medicare products

docs.title: How commission splits are calculated
docs.text: When a carrier pays a commission on a policy, AMS+ distributes that money among the agents in the policy's hierarchy according to the split rules defined on the policy.

docs.title: The split rules
docs.text:
Every policy has a hierarchy — a list of agents, each with a percentage. The percentages must sum to 100% before the policy can become active.

When a commission payment arrives, the platform multiplies the payment amount by each agent's percentage and creates a distribution record for each agent.

Example: A $1,000 commission with a 70/30 split produces $700 for the writing agent and $300 for their managing agent.

docs.title: Where splits come from
docs.text:
Splits can be set at two levels, applied in order:

  1. Policy-level split — set explicitly on the policy record. Takes precedence over everything else.
  2. Hierarchy default — the default split for the writing agent's agency hierarchy. Used when no policy-level split is defined.
flowchart LR
    A[Commission Received] --> B{Policy split defined?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use policy-level split]
    B -- No --> D[Use hierarchy default]
    C --> E[Create distribution records]
    D --> E

docs.title: Renewals and split changes
docs.text: When a policy renews, it uses the split that was active at renewal time — not the current default. Changing the hierarchy default after a policy is issued doesn't affect existing policies until they next renew.

docs.title: What happens when splits don't add up to 100%
docs.text: The platform enforces 100% totals. If a policy's splits don't sum to 100%, it's held in pending-splits status and the writing agent receives an alert. The commission payment is held until the split is corrected.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Commission splits directly determine agent income. Errors here create reconciliation work and affect agent trust in the platform.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Commissions overview
  • Commission reconciliation

docs.title: Commissions overview
docs.text:
Commissions are payments made by carriers to agents when a policy is sold, renewed, or maintained. AMS+ tracks every commission from carrier payment through distribution to individual agents.

This page explains the structure of how commissions work in AMS+. For calculation details, see How commission splits are calculated.

docs.title: What AMS+ tracks
docs.text:
AMS+ records commissions at two levels:

Carrier payments — the raw amount received from the carrier for a policy. These are imported via carrier statement uploads or entered manually.

Agent distributions — the portion of each carrier payment that flows to each agent in the policy's hierarchy. Calculated automatically from the split rules on the policy.

docs.title: Commission types
docs.text: Type When it's paid Typical rate New business When a new policy is issued Higher — incentivizes growth Renewal When an existing policy renews Lower than new business Override Paid to an upline agent on their downline's production Percentage of the downline's commission Bonus Carrier-specific production bonuses Varies widely

docs.title: When commissions are calculated
docs.text:
Commissions are calculated when a carrier payment is recorded against a policy. The platform matches the payment to the policy, looks up the active splits, and creates distribution records for each agent.

If the policy has no splits defined, the payment is held in pending status until splits are entered.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Accurate commission tracking is the core function most agencies rely on AMS+ for. Errors in commissions directly affect agent income and agency cash flow.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• How commission splits are calculated
  • Commission reconciliation
  • Record types reference

docs.title: Commission reconciliation
docs.text: Reconciliation is the process of verifying that the commissions recorded in AMS+ match the amounts received from carriers. Discrepancies — amounts that don't match or policies without a corresponding payment — are surfaced as reconciliation exceptions.

docs.title: How reconciliation works
docs.text:
Carriers send commission statements on a schedule (typically monthly). Agencies upload these statements into AMS+, and the platform matches each line to a policy and agent.

Matched lines — the carrier paid the expected amount for a known policy. These close automatically.

Unmatched lines — the carrier paid for a policy AMS+ doesn't recognize, or paid a different amount than expected. These require manual review.

Missing lines — AMS+ expected a payment (based on the policy's renewal schedule) but none appeared on the statement. These generate a follow-up alert.

docs.title: Common reconciliation exceptions
docs.text: Exception Typical cause Amount mismatch Rate change not yet reflected in AMS+; chargeback applied Unrecognized policy Policy number entered incorrectly; carrier issued new number Missing payment Policy lapsed at carrier; statement delivery delay Duplicate payment Carrier re-sent a prior statement; import run twice

docs.title: Resolving exceptions
docs.text:
For each exception, the agency can: - Match manually — link the exception to the correct policy. - Create an adjustment — record the difference as an adjustment for audit purposes. - Mark as waived — acknowledge the discrepancy without creating an adjustment (for small rounding differences).

All resolutions are logged with the resolving user and timestamp.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Unreconciled commissions mean agents may not be paid correctly — or the agency may be leaving carrier money unclaimed.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Commissions overview
  • How commission splits are calculated

docs.title: Compliance — overview
docs.text:
AMS+ includes compliance tooling that helps agencies meet their regulatory obligations — primarily around Errors & Omissions (E&O) documentation requirements and CMS rules for Medicare-related products. This page explains what the compliance area covers and why it matters.

High-risk area

Compliance documentation affects regulatory and legal obligations. Changes to pages in this area require review by both the compliance team and legal.

docs.title: What compliance means in AMS+
docs.text:
Insurance agencies face two main categories of compliance requirements:

E&O (Errors & Omissions) — agencies must maintain complete, accurate client and policy records to protect themselves from professional liability claims. AMS+ flags policies that are missing required fields before their effective date.

CMS rules — agents selling Medicare Advantage and Part D plans are subject to Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services regulations, including rules around scope-of-appointment documentation, enrollment timing windows, and required disclosures.

docs.title: How AMS+ surfaces compliance issues
docs.text:
AMS+ generates compliance alerts when:

  • A policy is approaching its effective date and required fields are missing.
  • A Medicare policy enrollment falls outside an allowed enrollment window.
  • A scope-of-appointment document is missing or expired for a client appointment.
  • A carrier sync reports a status mismatch between AMS+ and the carrier's system.

Alerts appear in the agency's compliance dashboard and can trigger email notifications to the responsible agent.

docs.title: The compliance alert lifecycle
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Trigger condition met] --> B[Alert created]
    B --> C{Resolved?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Alert closed with resolution note]
    C -- No, deadline passed --> E[Escalated]
    E --> F[Agency admin notified]

An alert is closed when the underlying condition is resolved — the missing field is filled in, the document is uploaded, or the issue is manually acknowledged by an admin with a reason code.

docs.title: Relationship to ConfigCat flags
docs.text: Some compliance rules are gated behind feature flags. For example, real-time CMS sync (cms-real-time-sync) is currently in staged rollout. Pages in this area will note when a described behavior is behind a flag and not yet live for all agencies.

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Unresolved compliance issues can result in carrier deactivation, CMS sanctions, or E&O claims — keeping this area accurate and current is a legal and financial obligation for the agencies using AMS+.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• Policies — overview
  • How commission splits are calculated

docs.title: Policies — overview
docs.text: A policy in AMS+ represents an insurance contract between a carrier and a client. This page explains what a policy record contains, how it moves through its lifecycle, and why the policies area is central to almost everything else in AMS+.

docs.title: What a policy is
docs.text:
When an agency submits an application to a carrier and it is approved, a policy is created in AMS+. The policy record is the system's authoritative record of:

  • Who is covered — the enrolled client(s) and their effective dates.
  • What is covered — the plan type, carrier, and benefit package.
  • Who sold it — the writing agent and any managing agents in the hierarchy.
  • How it pays — the commission schedule and split percentages.

Every other area in AMS+ — commissions, compliance, reporting — works from policy data. A policy record that is incomplete or miscategorized will cause downstream problems in all of these areas.

docs.title: The policy lifecycle
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Application submitted] --> B[Pending carrier approval]
    B --> C[Active]
    C --> D{Renewal?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Renewal pending]
    E --> C
    D -- No --> F[Lapsed / Cancelled]
  • Pending — the application has been submitted but the carrier has not yet confirmed coverage. No commissions are generated in this state.
  • Active — coverage is in force. Commission schedules are active. Compliance alerts may fire.
  • Renewal pending — the policy is approaching its anniversary date. The agency has the opportunity to review and re-submit.
  • Lapsed / Cancelled — coverage has ended. Historical commission records are retained.

docs.title: Why policies and carriers are linked
docs.text:
Every policy must be linked to a carrier record. The carrier record determines:

  • Which commission schedule applies (carriers pay different rates for different plan types).
  • Which compliance rules fire (CMS-regulated carriers have different E&O requirements).
  • Which carrier sync integration is active (some carriers send real-time status updates).

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: Accurate policy records are the foundation of accurate commissions, compliant client files, and reliable reporting — errors at the policy level compound across every downstream system.

docs.text:

docs.title: How-to guides
docs.text:
Focused guides for specific tasks in AMS+. Each guide answers "how do I accomplish X?" — it assumes you already know the basics and just need the steps.

Content coming in Phase 1 bootstrap. Priority guides: resolving E&O compliance alerts, processing a commission reconciliation, enrolling a Medicare Advantage policy.

docs.text:
Field-level reference for AMS+ record types. Each page documents what fields exist, what they mean, and what values are valid.

docs.title: Tutorials
docs.text:
Step-by-step walkthroughs for common AMS+ workflows. Each tutorial takes you from start to finish on a real task.

Content coming in Phase 1 bootstrap. Use Claude Code interactively to draft the first tutorials for the 3 seed business areas.
